Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909), entries 483–2370
FindAGrave memorial records

narrative_blocks.header: Married Abel Case on his birthday — September 22
narrative_blocks.body: Jennette Hooker married Abel Case on September 22, 1822 — Abel's 20th birthday, since he was born on September 22, 1802 — a coincidence mirrored by her sister Sarah, who six years later married on her own 20th birthday on March 11, 1828. The Case family moved from Middlebury, Vermont to Carmel, Michigan, and Jennette died at Charlotte, Michigan in 1873.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)
Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

narrative_blocks.header: Valentine's Day widow; daughter was ten days old
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Case's first wife Charlotte Creller died on February 14, 1857 — Valentine's Day — just ten days after their daughter Lottie was born on February 4, 1857, leaving the newborn without a mother. He married Lucy Wade in November 1859 and raised two more children with her.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

narrative_blocks.header: Married on her twenty-seventh birthday, February 11, 1868
narrative_blocks.body: Ellen Case was born on February 11, 1841, and married Charles Clay on that exact date twenty-seven years later — her twenty-seventh birthday. This is the third birthday wedding in a single family cluster from Middlebury, Vermont: her mother Jennette married on her husband's 20th birthday (1822), her aunt Sarah Hooker married on her own 20th birthday (1828), and now Ellen on her own 27th birthday (1868).

burial.plot_notes: Maple Hill Cemetery Charlotte Eaton Co MI per FindAGrave

narrative_blocks.header: Married on her twentieth birthday, March 11, 1828
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Hooker was born on March 11, 1808 in Middlebury, Vermont, and married Nelson Mead on that exact date twenty years later — her twentieth birthday — mirroring her sister Jennette, who in 1822 had married Abel Case on Abel's own 20th birthday. Sarah and Nelson moved west to Charlotte, Michigan, where she died in 1869 and he outlived her by twenty-seven years, dying in 1896.

burial.plot_notes: Hillside Cemetery State Center Iowa per FindAGrave

narrative_blocks.header: Married the founder of State Center, Iowa
narrative_blocks.body: Jennett Mead Barnes moved west to Iowa in 1856 with her husband William Barns, whose family pioneered State Center — he was the town's first Northwestern Railway station agent, built its first frame building, and donated the land for Hillside Cemetery. She died in Marshall, Michigan in 1923 at age eighty-six and was buried in State Center's Hillside Cemetery, the land her husband had given to the town.

narrative_blocks.header: Died twelve days before her forty-first birthday
narrative_blocks.body: Alta Mead Barrett died on April 14, 1882 in Charlotte, Michigan — just twelve days before her forty-first birthday on April 26. She left behind her husband Cornelius Barrett and at least two children, Fred and Harry, who were named in EH without dates, suggesting they were still young at the time of compilation.

Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909), entries 483–2370

narrative_blocks.body: Emeline Hooker married Stephen Gorham Mead on April 6, 1831 — the younger brother of Nelson Mead, who three years earlier had married her sister Sarah Hooker. Both pairs were sons and daughters of Middlebury, Vermont families whose lives intertwined through two simultaneous Hooker-Mead alliances before both couples moved west to Michigan.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

narrative_blocks.body: Louisa Hooker Mead married two physicians — Dr. Simeon Kendall Orr, who died in 1855 six years after their wedding, and Dr. Asa King Warren, who gave her four more children. Two of the Warren children died in infancy: May Louise at fifteen months in 1866, and Asa Gorham at twenty-one months in 1875.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

career.notes: Practiced law at Charlotte MI after Civil War service.

military_service.notes: Served in 7th Regiment Missouri Cavalry per EH #3778.

narrative_blocks.header: Named George Washington; Missouri cavalryman; became lawyer
narrative_blocks.body: George Washington Mead served in the 7th Regiment Missouri Cavalry during the Civil War, then settled afterward as a lawyer in Charlotte, Michigan — a trajectory from frontier soldier to professional that mirrors many of his generation. His first wife Adeline Corsett died in Elwood, Kansas in 1860, placing the young couple on the Kansas frontier before the war.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

career.notes: 'Physician and pharmacist at Olivet, Mich.' per EH #3779.

narrative_blocks.body: Charles Hooker Mead practiced medicine and pharmacy in Olivet, Michigan — the town where he was born in 1848 — becoming the third physician in his immediate family circle: his cousin Louisa Hooker Mead had married two doctors, Dr. Simeon Orr and Dr. Asa Warren. He married Ella Cole in January 1876; their daughter Gertrude Elvira was born later that same year.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

narrative_blocks.header: Born posthumously; died at sixteen in Olivet, Michigan
narrative_blocks.body: Junette Virginia Mead was born September 8, 1850 — four months after her father Stephen Gorham Mead died on April 28, 1850 — a posthumous child who never knew her father. She died at age sixteen on January 20, 1867 in Olivet, Michigan.
